conf-proc-title-group.conf-normalized-title: Energy Efficiency and Agricultural Engineering (EE&AE), 2025 10th International Conference on

conf-meta.conf-name: 2025 10th International Conference on Energy Efficiency and Agricultural Engineering (EE&AE)

title-group.article-title: Operational Issues in U140/U240/U241 Automatic Transmissions

institution-wrap.institution: Faculty of Mechanical Engineering and Technologies, Technical University of Varna

permissions.copyright-statement: 979-8-3315-1299-6 © 2025 IEEE. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining, and training of artificial intelligence and similar technologies. Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information.

abstract.p: The paper provides a comprehensive examination of the operational challenges associated with the $\mathrm{U} 140 / \mathrm{U} 240 / \mathrm{U} 241$ automatic transmissions. Identified are the primary defects that may arise during their use and analysed, further, are the causes of these issues, as well as the methods for their elimination. The performance characteristics of the pressure regulator solenoids are tested on a special bench that simulates the operational conditions within the transmission. Following repairs, these solenoids undergo further testing on the same bench to confirm their successful restoration and operational reliability.

sec.label: I.
sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Globally, the number of vehicles with automatic transmissions is steadily increasing. These transmissions are undergoing continuous development and improvements in control systems, and the number of gears has increased from three in the past to eight or more nowadays [1], [2]. After a certain mileage, especially in cases of improper maintenance, automatic transmissions begin to develop faults, making driving uncomfortable or even impossible [3].

The U140E/U240E/U241E automatic transmissions were commonly used in Toyota and Lexus vehicles from 1998 to 2013. These vehicles are powered by engines ranging from 1,8 to 3,0 liters in displacement, and can be found in models such as RAV4, MATRIX, HIGHLANDER, SOLARA, CELICA GTS, CAMRY, ES 300, RX 300, Scion, and Pontiac. The designation “E” indicates front-wheel drive configurations, while “F” denotes all-wheel drive ($4 \times 4$) systems. The analysed automatic transmission is fully electronic, with control managed by an electronic control module (PCM) that synchronises the gearbox's operation with other vehicle systems. The mechanical design of this transmission features three planetary gear sets, two one-way clutches, and six multi-plate clutches, three of which serve as brakes by connecting various components to the transmission housing. The main transmission components and the differential are typically positioned at the output section of the gearbox.
This transmission system comprises four forward gears and one reverse gear, along with a torque converter featuring a built-in “Lock up clutch” designed to enhance the vehicle's fuel efficiency.
To test the control unit of automatic transmissions, it is necessary to construct test benches [4]. There are studies focused on fuel economy in comparison with other types of transmissions [5].

Toyota vehicles are sold in the millions, with the majority equipped with automatic transmissions. However, there are very few publications addressing the operational issues of automatic transmissions and their diagnostics, which defines the focus of the current study.

Research objective: The primary objective of the current study is twofold:
1.
To identify mechanical defects that may arise during the operation of the gearbox. 2.
To assess the operational condition of the valve body and recommend effective repair methods. First, confirm that you have the correct template for your paper size.
Fig. 1.
Components of the gearbox

sec.label: II.
sec.title: Materials and Methods
sec.p: The surveyed gearbox is widely utilised in vehicles throughout the Republic of Bulgaria, particularly in models such as RAV4 and LEXUS. Despite the advanced age of these vehicles, many owners continued to rely on them due to their exceptional durability and reliability.
Extensive practical experience has been accumulated regarding the potential defects that can occur during the operation of these gearboxes, as well as the reliable methods for resolving such issues.

Figure 1 illustrates a cross-section of the automatic transmission, highlighting its constituent components. Table I details the implementation of the gears and lists the solenoids related to each gear.
Defects observed during operation can be classified into two primary groups, mechanical and electro-hydraulic failures.

sec.title: Mechanical Defects:
sec.p: 2.1.1. Low oil level, or a gearbox oil leak. Insufficient oil levels or leaks can result in clutch slippage and accelerated wear, ultimately necessitating gearbox repairs. Conversely, an excessively high oil level may also lead to defects due to overheating and oil foaming.

2.1.2. Improper filter selection during oil changes. There are two types of filters available for this transmission: one featuring a bag-type cleaning element and the other a meshtype cleaning element. In addition to the original equipment manufacturer filters, a variety of aftermarket filters are available at lower prices.

Nonetheless, incorporating a bag-type cleaning filter may cause some fine threads to dislodge from certain gearbox components within designated transmission situations. This detachment is often undetectable, as the threads are extremely fine and microscopic.
Fig. 2.
On the left - bag-type filter, on the right - mesh-type filter

2.1.3. Wear of the „Lock-up clutch” in the torque converter. The deterioration of the lock-up clutch in the torque converter can be attributed to several factors, including excessive vehicle mileage, antifreeze leaking into the transmission, inappropriate oil usage for this specific type of transmission, and not adhering to recommended oil change schedules.
2.1.4. Mechanical defects within the transmission may also include damage to the planetary mechanisms.

This issue often arises from neglecting to change the oil, which leads to a loss of its lubricating properties. Additionally, wear on the bearing bushings located on the shafts can diminish or eliminate the oil pressure necessary for lubricating the planetary mechanisms.
2.1.5. Wear of clutch “C2”.

Clutch C2 may exhibit defects for two primary reasons. First, wear may develop within the two channels located in the rear casing of the transmission, where the segments facilitating the provision of oil to engage the clutch are housed.
Table I. Components involved in gear implementation.
Lever Position Gear Solenoid Valve $\mathbf{C}_{\mathbf{1}}$ $\mathbf{C}_{\mathbf{2}}$ $\mathbf{C}_{\mathbf{3}}$ $\mathbf{B}_{\mathbf{1}}$ $\mathbf{B}_{\mathbf{2}}$ $\mathbf{B}_{\mathbf{3}}$ $\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{1}}$ $\mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{2}}$
SL1 SL2 S4 DSL
P Park ON ON OFF OFF
O
R Reverse ON OFF OFF OFF
O
O O
N Neutral ON ON OFF OFF
O
D 1st ON ON OFF OFF O
O O O
2nd OFF ON OFF OFF O
O
O
O
3rd OFF OFF OFF OFF/ON* O O
O
O
4th OFF OFF ON OFF/ON* O O O
2 1st ON ON OFF OFF O
O O O
2nd OFF ON OFF OFF O
O
O
O
L 1st ON ON OFF ON O
O O O O

The primary defect identified involves the infiltration of particulate matter into the solenoids and valve body of the transmission system. Following just a modest amount of mileage, the solenoids' control components, namely the pressure regulators, start to fail, causing subsequently improper pressure regulation within the transmission.

Throughout actual road-testing scenarios, this malfunction manifests as jarring gear shifts or an unanticipated surge in engine speed during gear transitions. Additionally, drivers may experience a loss of specific gears, which can lead to transmission entering an emergency mode.
Figure 2 exemplifies the two types of filters involved in this process.
Second, the piston that applies pressure to the clutch plates may also experience wear. Replacing this piston with a new part is crucial whenever the transmission undergoes repairs [11].
2.1.6. All of the aforementioned mechanical issues might stem from an inadequate operation of the valve body- [7], [13].

sec.title: Electrohydraulic Issues:
sec.p: 2.2.1. Given that gear shifting is electronically controlled, it is essential to utilize an electronic tester to retrieve any error codes stored in the memory of the electronic control module. This diagnostic step must be completed prior to initiating any repair work. Should any electrical components be found defective, they must be replaced immediately to ensure optimal functionality. - [8].

2.2.2. Evidence suggests that the transmission control module “TCM” is susceptible to frequent faults. These issues typically manifest as delays and harsh shifting when engaging the reverse gear, as well as during transitions from second to third gear. Continuing to operate the vehicle under these conditions can lead to mechanical failures within the gearbox, necessitating a comprehensive overhaul. It is imperative to either repair or replace the TCM module without delay; failure to do so may result in recurring defects within one to two weeks post-repair.

In older vehicle models, the TCM can be disassembled, allowing for the replacement of its electronic components. However, in newer models, the TCM is encapsulated in resin, rendering it non-disassemblable and thus impossible to repair.

2.2.3. Wear in specific holes within the valve body can lead to altered pressures in certain control circuits, resulting in erratic gearbox behaviour. To address this issue, the valve body must be disassembled and thoroughly cleaned. The two blocks are then reassembled separately, with every opening undergoing evaluation via a vacuum wear tester. Any worn holes are reamed using specialised reamers and fixtures from “Sonnax”, and repair pistons and springs from the same manufacturer are installed [9].

It should be emphasized that the control components normally exhibit minimal wear. The most susceptible part is the opening of the “solenoid modulator” valve, which regulates the pressure supplied to the solenoids.

For reference purposes, the two types of valve bodies are illustrated in Figure 3.
Fig. 3.
At the left side - model years 1998-2005, at the right -2006-2013
2.2.4. Defects in solenoids. Solenoids can experience two primary types of defects: electrical and mechanical.
2.2.4.1. Electrical defects are typically identified using an electronic tester, which can pinpoint the specific solenoid that is malfunctioning.

2.2.4.2. Mechanical issues are detected when solenoids are removed from the valve body for inspection. The S4 and DSL solenoids are classified as “ON - OFF” types and are “normally closed”. They can be tested using a 12 -volt power supply. In contrast, the SLT, SL1 and SL2 solenoids function as pressure regulators and operate on a “Duty cycle” system. Duty cycle is the fraction of a time period a system or signal is active, or “on,” compared to its total period, typically expressed as a percentage. Testing for these solenoids must be conducted on a specialised bench that simulates the operational conditions within the gearbox, with a frequency of 300 Hz.

To that effect, two regulators were selected from a total of six solenoids acquired from two different gearboxes. Both gearboxes exhibited mechanical defects, including worn clutches that required repair. It should be pointed out that the valve body of the first gearbox showed no unacceptable wear, while the bore of the “solenoid modulator” valve in the second gearbox was significantly worn. The control and improvement of designs depend on our experience in maintenance, failures and repairs of previous generations of automatic transmissions [12], [14].

sec.label: III.
sec.title: Experimental Results:
sec.p: The solenoids were tested on the bench- [6] under two conditions: with cold oil and with oil at operating temperature. The results of pressure [bar] tests are summarised in Tables II and III, and the corresponding graphs are illustrated in Figures 4 and 5.

Following a detailed examination of the results obtained from testing the solenoids on the bench under both ambient and operating temperature settings, the following conclusions have been reached:3.1. At room temperature (with cold oil), the solenoids exhibited characteristics that aligned closely with their expected performance curves. It is worth mentioning, though, that some solenoids displayed significant deviations from the required specifications.

3.2. By contrast, at the operating temperature, even larger deviations from the desired characteristics are observed. Specifically, solenoids SLT, SL1 and SL1-1 fail to close properly, resulting in residual pressure that hinders the effective operation of the control system. In addition, solenoid L2 functions within certain percentage limits, which adversely affects the transmission overall performance.
Table III. Experimental results operating temperature t=(95-100)0c
Duty cycle Designation of solenoids
% SLT SL1 SL2 SLT-1 SL1-1 SL2-1
0 7,77 7,33 8,44 7,86 6,61 7,55
4 7,64 7,28 8,44 7,81 6,61 7,55
9 7,59 7,08 8,24 7,61 6,44 7,55
15 6,70 6,15 6,95 6,75 6,24 7,04
20 6,26 5,84 6,91 6,04 5,73 6,44
25 5,64 5,15 5,97 5,11 5,26 5,46
29 4,97 4,42 5,11 4,22 4,71 4,66
34 4,35 3,64 2,49 3,11 4,08 3,77
40 3,53 2,49 2,49 1,62 3,22 2,73
45 2,73 1,80 2,49 0,38 2,38 1,78
50 1,66 1,44 0,49 0,18 1,49 0,62
54 1,22 1,33 0,47 0,16 0,95 0,20
59 1,18 1,33 0,47 0,16 0,90 0,18
65 1,15 1,33 0,20 0,16 0,90 0,18
Fig. 5.
Characteristics of solenoids at operating temperature t=(95-100)°c

To address these issues, the solenoids are disassembled using a specialised fixture, repaired and subsequently reassembled with the same precision device. Following the repairs, the solenoids are retested on the bench at both room and operating temperatures. The results of these tests are presented in Table IV, with a graphical representation provided in Figure 6. It is important to note that the results discussed herein pertain solely to the operating temperature; at room temperature, the solenoids demonstrate optimal characteristics.

Practical experience indicates that the transmission performs relatively well with cold oil. However, as the oil temperature within the system increases, the performance of the transmission tends to deteriorate. This decline is manifested through harder gear shifts, delayed shifting responses, and an increase in engine crankshaft rotation frequency during gear changes.
Table IV. Experimental results after repair
Duty cycle Designation of solenoids
% SLT SL1 SL2 SLT-1 SL1-1 SL2-1
0 7,50 7,72 7,44 7,81 8,04 7,88
4 7,50 7,66 7,35 7,79 7,99 7,88
9 7,35 7,37 7,24 7,50 7,72 7,59
15 6,84 6,88 6,61 7,19 7,37 7,28
20 6,28 6,46 6,15 6,61 6,77 6,66
25 5,73 5,88 5,48 5,99 6,22 6,10
29 5,04 5,19 4,73 5,46 5,59 5,55
34 4,33 4,44 4,17 4,71 4,97 4,79
40 3,60 3,84 3,40 3,97 4,11 4,04
45 2,82 3,00 2,51 3,20 3,35 3,24
50 1,84 2,00 1,66 2,18 2,22 2,22
54 0,91 1,11 0,53 0,71 0,73 0,60
59 0,27 0,22 0,18 0,16 0,22 0,18
65 0,18 0,16 0,13 0,16 0,18 0,13
Fig. 6.
Characteristics at operating temperature - following repair at t = (95-100)°c

sec.label: IV.
sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The failure of these gearboxes may be attributed to several factors, including the selection of an unsuitable filter, the use of oil that does not meet the specifications for this transmission, or an incorrectly set oil level - either insufficiently low or excessively high.
When replacing the valve body, it is crucial to install a valve body that is specifically compatible with the specifications of this particular transmission model.
The valve body should be thoroughly cleaned and inspected for wear using a vacuum test. Any worn components should be repaired to ensure optimal performance.

Should there be delays or challenges in shifting into reverse and the second or third gears, it is advisable to consider repairing the transmission control module if feasible, or replacing it with a new unit. This proactive measure can prevent potential mechanical failures within the transmission.

Pressure regulator solenoids must be bench-tested to verify their proper functionality. Any malfunctioning solenoid ought to be mended if possible or replaced with new ones to ensure system reliability.
Adhering to proper maintenance protocols and executing appropriate repairs on these transmissions will ensure a longlasting and trouble-free service for the vehicle owner.

ref.mixed-citation: A. Moskalik, M. Stuhldreher and J. Kargul, “Benchmarking a 2018 Toyota Camry UB80E eight-speed automatic transmission,” SAE Technical Papers, 2020-April, DOI: https://doi.org/10.4271/2020-011286.
